title: Semantic Communication Through the Lens of Context-Dependent Channel Modeling

sections.text: Encoder 𝑝(𝑥|𝑠, 𝑞!) Semantic Decoder 𝑊 𝑆! 𝑋! 𝑊$ 𝑝𝑞", 𝑢, 𝑠, 𝑥 = 𝑝𝑞" 𝑝𝑢𝑞" 𝑝𝑠𝑢, 𝑞" 𝑝(𝑥|𝑠, 𝑞") 𝑄! " 𝑄" ! 𝑄# ! Fig. 2. Semantic channel modeled as a context-dependent channel. C. Representationally Capable Semantic Sender In [9], the impact of semantic noise at the transmitter, which affects its representational capability, was examined. To simplify notation, the context was omitted from the mathemat- ical formulations. In this subsection, we revisit this problem shortly through the lens of a state-dependent channel model depicted in Figure 2. Speciﬁcally, the semantic sender can be modeled as a state-dependent channel, where the channel state informationcorresponding to the contextis assumed to be non-causally available at both the transmitter and the receiver, e.g., Qn 2 = Qn 1. Consistent with the results in [9], we establish that a semantic sender can express itself for itself without ambiguity and is representationally capable if and only if its semantic rate does not exceed its representational capacity. Theorem 1: The semantic sender illustrated in Figure 2, is representationally capable if and only if the semantic information rate does not exceed its representational capacity: CRC = max p(s|q1) I(S; X|Q1). (3) The proof follows a similar approach to that of the capacity analysis for state-dependent DMC, as presented in [18]. In the following, we provide an outline of the achievability part of the proof: • Generate 2n ˜ R independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) auxiliary sequences U n according to the p.m.f. p(u). • Partition the set of 2n ˜ R sequences U n into 2nR equal- sized bins. Therefore, each bin contains 2n( ˜ R−R) se- quences U n. • Associate each semantic w to a bin. • For the semantic w and the context qn 1 , look for a codeword un inside the associated bin with w, such that: (un, qn 1 ) ∈T n ϵ (U, Q1). (4) • The probability of not ﬁnding such a sequence un inside the bin asymptotically tends to zero, if: ˜R −R > I(U; Q1). (5) • Assume p(s|u, q1) is a deterministic function f. There- fore, given un and qn 1 , the semantic encoder generates the semantic codeword sn according to s = f(u, q1). • Given the semantic codeword sn and the context qn 1 , the semantic source express itself via a message codeword Xn associated with a message M, according to the conditional p.m.f. p(x|s, q1). • Given the context qn 2 = qn 1 and the received message codeword xn, the semantic decoder looks for a unique un, such that: (un, xn, , qn 1 ) ∈T n ϵ (U, X, Q1). (6) • The probability of not ﬁnding such a unique sequence un asymptotically tends to zero, if: ˜R < I(U; X, Q1). (7) • Combining (5) and (7), the semantic source is unam- biguously expressible, and therefore is representational capable, if: R < I(U; X|Q1). (8) • Due to the Markov chain U →S →X conditioned on q1: R < I(U; X|Q1) ≤I(S; X|Q1). (9) D. Semantic Communication over a Semantic Channel As mentioned earlier, the model illustrated in Figure 2 can represent not only the semantic sender for analyzing its repre- sentational capability, but also a semantic communication sce- nario where semantic noise arises solely from the discrepancy between the transmitter’s and receiver’s background knowl- edge. This discrepancy is modeled through the availability of context information, while the absence of physical channel noise ensures that Xn is directly received without distortion. In what follows, we analyze three possible scenarios: (1) the sender and receiver have similar background knowledge, (2) the receivers background knowledge is a subset of the senders knowledge, (3) the senders background knowledge is a subset of the receivers knowledge, and (4) the Sender and receiver share partial background Knowledge. 1) Sender and Receiver with Similar Background Knowl- edge: Consider the scenario where the sender and the receiver have the same context information. Theorem 2: The capacity of the semantic channel depicted in Figure 2, which accounts for semantic noise while assum- ing a noiseless physical channel, in the scenario where the sender and the receiver have the same context information, e.g., Qn 2 = Qn 1, is given by: C = max p(s|q1) I(S; X|Q1). (10) The proof follows a similar approach to that of the capacity analysis for state-dependent DMC, as presented in [18]. The outline of the achievability proof follows exactly the same structure as that of Theorem 1. 2) Receivers Background Knowledge as a Subset of the senders Knowledge: Consider the scenario where the re- ceiver’s context information is already known to the sender, as depicted in Figure 3analogous to a mother communicating with her little child, where the mother is aware of the child’s limited contextual understanding and adjusts her communica- tion accordingly.

Encoder 𝑝(𝑥|𝑠, 𝑞!, 𝑞(") Semantic Decoder 𝑊 𝑆! 𝑋! 𝑊$ 𝑄" # = (𝑄! #, 𝑄," #) 𝑄" ! = (𝑄# !, 𝑄)" !) 𝑄$ ! = 𝑄# ! 𝑝𝑞#, 𝑞-", 𝑢, 𝑠, 𝑥= 𝑝𝑞#, 𝑞-" 𝑝𝑢𝑞#, 𝑞-" 𝑝𝑠𝑢, 𝑞#, 𝑞-" 𝑝(𝑥|𝑠, 𝑞#, 𝑞-") Fig. 3. Semantic channel modeled as a context-dependent channel, where the semantic receiver’s background knowledge is a subset of the semantic sender’s knowledge. Theorem 3: The capacity of the semantic channel depicted in Figure 3, which accounts for semantic noise while as- suming a noiseless physical channel, in the scenario where the receiver’s context information is already known to the transmitter, e.g., Qn 1 = (Qn 0, ˜Qn 1) and Qn 2 = Qn 0, is given by: C = I(U; X|Q2) −I(U; ˜Q1|Q0). (11) The proof follows a similar approach to that of the capacity analysis for state-dependent DMC, as presented in [18]. In the following, we provide an outline of the achievability part of the proof: • Generate 2n ˜ R i.i.d. auxiliary sequences U n according to the p.m.f. p(u). • Partition the set of 2n ˜ R sequences U n into 2nR equal- sized bins. Therefore, each bin contains 2n( ˜ R−R) se- quences U n. • Associate each semantic w to a bin. • For the semantic w and the context qn 1 = (qn 0 , ˜qn 1 ), look for a codeword un inside the associated bin with w, such that: (un, qn 0 , ˜qn 1 ) ∈T n ϵ (U, Q0, ˜Q1). (12) • The probability of not ﬁnding such a sequence un inside the bin asymptotically tends to zero, if: ˜R −R > I(U; Q0, ˜Q1). (13) • Assume p(s|u, q0, ˜q1) is a deterministic function f. Therefore, given un and (qn 0 , ˜qn 1 ), the semantic encoder generates the semantic codeword sn according to s = f(u, q0, ˜q1). • Given the semantic codeword sn and the context (qn 0 , ˜qn 1 ), the semantic source express itself via a message codeword Xn associated with a message M, according to the conditional p.m.f. p(x|s, q0, ˜q1). • Given the context qn 2 = qn 0 and the received message codeword xn, the semantic decoder looks for a unique un, such that: (un, xn, , qn 0 ) ∈T n ϵ (U, X, Q0). (14) • The probability of not ﬁnding such a unique sequence un asymptotically tends to zero, if: ˜R < I(U; X, Q0). (15)

Encoder 𝑝(𝑥|𝑠, 𝑞!) Semantic Decoder 𝑊 𝑆! 𝑋! 𝑊$ 𝑄" # = 𝑄! # 𝑄" ! = 𝑄# ! 𝑝𝑞#, 𝑢, 𝑠, 𝑥= 𝑝𝑞# 𝑝𝑢𝑞# 𝑝𝑠𝑢, 𝑞# 𝑝(𝑥|𝑠, 𝑞#) 𝑄$ ! = (𝑄# !, 𝑄1$ !) Fig. 4. Semantic channel modeled as a context-dependent channel, where the semantic senders background knowledge is a subset of the semantic receivers knowledge. • Combining (13) and (15), the semantic source is unam- biguously expressible, if: R < I(U; X|Q0) −I(U; ˜Q1|Q0), (16) which, because Q2 = Q0, simpliﬁes to: R < I(U; X|Q2) −I(U; ˜Q1|Q0). (17) • Due to the Markov chain U →S →X conditioned on (q0, ˜q1): R < I(S; X|Q2) −I(U; ˜Q1|Q0) (18) 3) Senders Background Knowledge as a Subset of the Re- ceivers Knowledge: Consider the scenario where the sender’s context information is already known to the receiver, as depicted in Figure 4analogous to a little child communicating with the mother, where the mother is aware of the child’s limited contextual understanding and adjusts her interpretation accordingly, enabling her to comprehend the intended mean- ing even though the childs speech may appear nonsensical to others. Theorem 4: For the semantic channel depicted in Figure 4, which accounts for semantic noise while assuming a noiseless physical channel, in the scenario where the sender’s context information is already known to the receiver, e.g., Qn 1 = Qn 0 and Qn 2 = (Qn 0, ˜Qn 2), the following rate is achievable: R < I(U; X|Q2) + I(U; ˜Q2|Q0). (19) The proof is inspired by the similar approach to the capacity analysis of state-dependent DMC, as presented in [18]. In the following, we provide an outline of the proof: • Generate 2n ˜ R i.i.d. auxiliary sequences U n according to the p.m.f. p(u). • Partition the set of 2n ˜ R sequences U n into 2nR equal- sized bins. Therefore, each bin contains 2n( ˜ R−R) se- quences U n. • Associate each semantic w to a bin. • For the semantic w and the context qn 1 = qn 0 , look for a codeword un inside the associated bin with w, such that: (un, qn 0 ) ∈T n ϵ (U, Q0). (20) • The probability of not ﬁnding such a sequence un inside the bin asymptotically tends to zero, if: ˜R −R > I(U; Q0). (21)

Encoder 𝑝(𝑥|𝑠, 𝑞!, 𝑞(") Semantic Decoder 𝑊 𝑆! 𝑋! 𝑊$ 𝑄" # = (𝑄! #, 𝑄," #) 𝑄" ! = (𝑄# !, 𝑄)" !) 𝑝𝑞#, 𝑞-", 𝑢, 𝑠, 𝑥= 𝑝𝑞#, 𝑞-" 𝑝𝑢𝑞#, 𝑞-" 𝑝𝑠𝑢, 𝑞#, 𝑞-" 𝑝(𝑥|𝑠, 𝑞#, 𝑞-") 𝑄$ ! = (𝑄# !, 𝑄)$ !) Fig. 5. Semantic channel modeled as a context-dependent channel, where the semantic sender and receiver share partial background knowledge. • Assume p(s|u, q0) is a deterministic function f. There- fore, given un and qn 0 , the semantic encoder generates the semantic codeword sn according to s = f(u, q0). • Given the semantic codeword sn and the context qn 0 , the semantic source express itself via a message codeword Xn associated with a message M, according to the conditional p.m.f. p(x|s, q0). • Given the context qn 2 = (qn 0 , ˜qn 2 ) and the received message codeword xn, the semantic decoder looks for a unique un, such that: (un, xn, , qn 0 , ˜qn 2 ) ∈T n ϵ (U, X, Q0, ˜Q2). (22) • The probability of not ﬁnding such a unique sequence un asymptotically tends to zero, if: ˜R < I(U; X, Q0, ˜Q2). (23) • Combining (21) and (23), the semantic source is unam- biguously expressible, if: R < I(U; X|Q0, ˜Q2) + I(U; ˜Q2|Q0), (24) which, because Q2 = (Q0, ˜Q2), simpliﬁes to: R < I(U; X|Q2) + I(U; ˜Q2|Q0). (25) 4) Sender and Receiver Share Partial Background Knowl- edge: Consider the general scenario where the sender and receiver share partial context information, as depicted in Figure 5. Theorem 5: For the semantic channel depicted in Figure 5, which accounts for semantic noise while assuming a noiseless physical channel, in the general scenario where the sender and receiver share partial context information Qn 0, which means that, Qn 1 = (Qn 0, ˜Qn 1) and Qn 2 = (Qn 0, ˜Qn 2), the following rate is achievable: R < I(U; X|Q2) + I(U; ˜Q2|Q0) −I(U; ˜Q1|Q0). (26) The proof is inspired by the similar approach to the capacity analysis of state-dependent DMC, as presented in [18]. In the following, we provide an outline of the proof: • Generate 2n ˜ R i.i.d. auxiliary sequences U n according to the p.m.f. p(u). • Partition the set of 2n ˜ R sequences U n into 2nR equal- sized bins. Therefore, each bin contains 2n( ˜ R−R) se- quences U n. • Associate each semantic w to a bin. • For the semantic w and the context qn 1 = (qn 0 , ˜qn 1 ), look for a codeword un inside the associated bin with w, such that: (un, qn 0 , ˜qn 1 ) ∈T n ϵ (U, Q0, ˜Q1). (27) • The probability of not ﬁnding such a sequence un inside the bin asymptotically tends to zero, if: ˜R −R > I(U; Q0, ˜Q1). (28) • Assume p(s|u, q0, ˜q1) is a deterministic function f. Therefore, given un and (qn 0 , ˜qn 1 ), the semantic encoder generates the semantic codeword sn according to s = f(u, q0, ˜q1). • Given the semantic codeword sn and the context (qn 0 , ˜qn 1 ), the semantic source express itself via a message codeword Xn associated with a message M, according to the conditional p.m.f. p(x|s, q0, ˜q1). • Given the context qn 2 = (qn 0 , ˜qn 2 ) and the received message codeword xn, the semantic decoder looks for a unique un, such that: (un, xn, , qn 0 , ˜qn 2 ) ∈T n ϵ (U, X, Q0, ˜Q2). (29) • The probability of not ﬁnding such a unique sequence un asymptotically tends to zero, if: ˜R < I(U; X, Q0, ˜Q2). (30) • Combining (28) and (30), the semantic source is unam- biguously expressible, if: R < I(U; X|Q0, ˜Q2) + I(U; ˜Q2|Q0) −I(U; ˜Q1|Q0), (31) which, because Q2 = (Q0, ˜Q2), simpliﬁes to: R < I(U; X|Q2) + I(U; ˜Q2|Q0) −I(U; ˜Q1|Q0). (32) In summary, for various conﬁgurations of background knowledge, the following achievable rates are derived: 1) Sender and receiver have similar knowledge: R < I(U; X|Q2), (33) 2) When the receiver’s knowledge is a subset of the sender’s, the sender needs to adjust the communication by transmitting at a lower rate compared to the case in (33), in order to remain understandable to the receiver. R < I(U; X|Q2) −I(U; ˜Q1|Q0). (34) 3) When the sender’s knowledge is a subset of the re- ceiver’s, the receiver can adapt the interpretation accord- ingly, allowing the sender to communicate at a higher rate compared to the case in (33): R < I(U; X|Q2) + I(U; ˜Q2|Q0). (35) 4) When the sender and receiver share partial knowledge, the achievable communication ratecompared to the case in (33)can be either lower or higher, depending on the nature of their shared and private knowledge. R < I(U; X|Q2)+I(U; ˜Q2|Q0)−I(U; ˜Q1|Q0). (36) III. CONCLUSION This paper investigated the evolving paradigm of semantic communication. In this paper, we explored a speciﬁc subclass of semantic communication problems, where semantic noise arises solely from the semantic channel, assuming a noiseless physical channel. Building on the probabilistic model of semantic communication and leveraging the role of context, we proposed a virtual state-dependent channel model to cap- ture the impact of contextual information on communication. Through this model, we analyzed the representational capabil- ity of the semantic encoder and examined different semantic communication scenarios affected by semantic noise. Finally, we derived the capacity results for these scenarios, providing fundamental insights into reliable semantic communication in the absence of physical channel noise. These ﬁndings enhance our understanding of semantic communication and provide a foundation for future research aimed at unlocking its full potential for next-generation communication networks.
